refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal and comparative nature of the requirement. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (maintain), specifies the comparison logic (at or above target), and anchors the measurement window (evaluation period). This remains solution-neutral by not prescribing redundancy, protocol choice, or network architecture—only the outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'customer-defined target threshold' preserves flexibility while making the acceptance criterion explicit and measurable.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (total_uptime / total_period_duration). Compare against the customer-defined target (e.g., 0.999). Pass if measured availability ≥ target; fail otherwise. Document any outages, root causes, and recovery times to support root-cause analysis.
sub_frs: Establish and document the customer-defined teleoperation link availability target threshold and evaluation period duration before system deployment.
Monitor teleoperation link status (up/down) continuously during operation.
Record all teleoperation link outages with timestamps and duration.
Calculate cumulative teleoperation link availability over the evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative communication channels before loss of availability occurs.

DP3: Establish hierarchical communication architecture with local autonomous fallback mode that preserves critical teleoperation functions during link unavailability, resuming remote control upon link restoration.
DP4: Implement adaptive bandwidth management and protocol selection that dynamically adjusts transmission parameters to maintain minimum viable link availability under varying network conditions.

DP5: Combine redundant pathways (DP1) with quality monitoring (DP2) and autonomous fallback (DP3) in an integrated availability assurance system with coordinated state management across all three mechanisms.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Integrated availability assurance system combining (1) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, (2) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting, and (3) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback mode that preserves critical functions during link loss and resumes remote control upon restoration.

potential_sources: State synchronization between redundant pathways and failover logic must be decoupled via clear handoff protocols to avoid race conditions.
Monitoring system (DP2) and failover trigger logic (DP1) require shared link quality metrics; decouple via published quality thresholds rather than direct component coupling.
Local autonomous fallback (DP3) and remote teleoperation control must maintain independent command stacks to prevent interference; use clear mode-switching boundaries.
Pathway selection logic must not depend on specific pathway implementation details; abstract via pathway capability contracts (latency, reliability, bandwidth).
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is composite but achievable with low coupling if each sub-mechanism (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) is designed as independently testable and replaceable. The integration points are well-defined: (a) failover trigger interface, (b) quality metric publication, (c) mode-switch protocol, (d) pathway abstraction layer. No inherent logical contradiction; coupling risk is primarily architectural (implementation module boundaries) rather than functional.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: At least two independent communication pathways are available at the worksite (e.g., cellular, satellite, mesh, wired backup). RISK: If only one pathway exists, redundancy cannot be implemented; fallback to DP3 (autonomous mode) alone.

ASSUMPTION: Link quality can be measured or inferred in real-time with sufficient fidelity to predict degradation before loss. RISK: Noisy or delayed quality signals may trigger false failovers or miss genuine degradation; requires tuning of detection thresholds.

ASSUMPTION: Local autonomous fallback mode is operationally acceptable and safe under worksite safety plan. RISK: If safety plan prohibits autonomous operation, DP3 cannot be used; availability must rely solely on DP1+DP2.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to capture representative network variability and validate 0.999 availability claim. RISK: Short evaluation periods may not expose rare failure modes; statistical confidence in target may be low.

ASSUMPTION: Failover and mode-switching latency (seconds to tens of seconds) is acceptable to operator and worksite safety plan. RISK: If sub-second failover is required, DP5 architecture may not meet latency constraints.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target threshold is technically achievable given worksite network infrastructure. RISK: If infrastructure is inherently unreliable (e.g., <0.99 baseline), 0.999 target may be unattainable without major infrastructure investment outside system scope.
ASSUMPTION: Operator training and procedures account for mode transitions and autonomous fallback behavior. RISK: Inadequate training may lead to unsafe operation during failover events.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) acceptable failover latency and mode-switching behavior, (2) whether local autonomous fallback is operationally permitted, (3) minimum number of independent pathways available, (4) definition of 'link availability' (e.g., does it include failover transition time?), and (5) statistical confidence level for the 0.999 target over the evaluation period. Additionally, clarify whether 0.999 applies to the teleoperation link alone or includes the broader system (e.g., sensor feedback, command execution). Current FR is sound but would benefit from these boundary conditions to validate DP5 feasibility and guide IM (implementation module) design.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all remote control communication pathways, link quality monitoring, failover logic, and autonomous fallback modes that preserve critical vehicle functions during connectivity loss and enable seamless resumption of remote control upon restoration.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with physical network infrastructure; Teleoperation monitors link quality, detects degradation, triggers failover to redundant pathways, and receives restoration signals.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives remote operator commands and mission directives; Teleoperation translates these into vehicle control signals, queues them during link loss, and executes queued tasks upon restoration.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and link quality metrics back to remote operator; includes predictive degradation indicators and autonomous fallback status.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and critical function definitions from Safety Services; Teleoperation ensures critical functions remain executable in autonomous fallback mode and escalates safety violations.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Teleoperation is the natural home for redundant communication pathways, link quality monitoring, failover logic, and autonomous fallback modes. The DP is fundamentally about preserving and restoring remote control capability. Minor diffusion: safety constraint definitions originate in Safety Services, and vehicle actuation execution occurs in Vehicle module, but control logic and failover orchestration remain cleanly contained.

Poor fit. Vehicle module owns physical actuation and autonomous navigation, but the DP is primarily about communication assurance and remote control continuity, not vehicle dynamics. Placing redundant pathways, link monitoring, and failover logic in Vehicle would scatter teleoperation concerns across two modules and create tight coupling between communication and vehicle control.

Poor fit. Safety Services owns safety policy and constraint enforcement, but the DP is about communication resilience and control continuity, not safety rule definition. Placing failover and link monitoring in Safety Services conflates safety policy with communication infrastructure and creates unnecessary coupling.

Very poor fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Assigning the DP to NGHE Solution would bypass architectural layering and prevent clear ownership. The DP requires a specific, focused module boundary to maintain cohesion and enable independent testing and evolution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the correct architectural home for DP5 because: (1) redundant communication pathways and failover logic are intrinsic to remote control capability; (2) link quality monitoring and predictive degradation detection directly support the teleoperation use case; (3) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback preserves critical functions when remote control is unavailable, then resumes remote control upon restoration—a core teleoperation concern. The module boundary cleanly separates communication assurance (Teleoperation) from vehicle actuation (Vehicle) and safety policy (Safety Services), enabling independent evolution and testing.
containment_risks: Risk: Safety Services must define which functions are 'critical' and executable in autonomous fallback mode. Teleoperation must respect these constraints without owning safety policy. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface carries safety constraint definitions; Teleoperation queries and enforces them but does not author them.

Risk: Vehicle module must execute queued commands and autonomous fallback actions. Teleoperation must not duplicate vehicle control logic. Mitigation: Teleoperation sends high-level control directives (e.g., 'move forward 5m') to Vehicle; Vehicle owns low-level actuation and dynamics.

Risk: Network Link infrastructure (redundant pathways, physical failover) may be owned by external network provider or platform. Teleoperation must abstract this complexity. Mitigation: Network Link interface provides a unified view of available pathways and quality metrics; Teleoperation selects and switches pathways without managing physical infrastructure.

Risk: Predictive degradation detection may require machine learning or statistical models. If these models are shared across multiple modules, diffusion increases. Mitigation: Encapsulate prediction logic within Teleoperation; expose only link quality and degradation alerts via Telemetry interface.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable target (link availability ≥ 0.999 over agreed evaluation period). The IM boundary (Teleoperation module) owns all mechanisms required to achieve this: redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, failover orchestration, and autonomous fallback. Verification can be conducted via: (1) continuous monitoring of link availability metrics during evaluation period, (2) failover testing under simulated degradation scenarios, (3) autonomous fallback mode validation against safety constraints. The Telemetry interface explicitly exports link quality metrics and degradation indicators, enabling direct measurement against acceptance criteria.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR->DP->IM chain is clear and traceable. FR (maintain availability at target threshold) maps to DP (integrated availability assurance combining redundancy, monitoring, and fallback) maps to IM (Teleoperation module with defined scope and four external interfaces). Ownership is unambiguous: Teleoperation owns communication resilience; Vehicle owns actuation; Safety Services owns constraint definitions. Interface contracts are explicit (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert), enabling independent development and testing.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly and sufficiently addresses FR. The three pillars of DP5 (redundant pathways with failover, real-time monitoring with predictive detection, hierarchical architecture with autonomous fallback) are necessary and jointly sufficient to maintain link availability above target threshold. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific technologies) while being concrete enough to guide architecture. No logical gaps or contradictions detected between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Predictive degradation detection logic (part of DP5 pillar 2) could diffuse if machine learning models or statistical algorithms are duplicated across Teleoperation and other modules (e.g., Vehicle autonomous navigation, Safety Services anomaly detection). Mitigation: Encapsulate all prediction logic within Teleoperation; expose only high-level alerts (link quality score, degradation probability) via Telemetry interface. If shared models are needed, create a separate Analytics module and consume via dependency injection.

Risk: Safety constraint definitions (which functions are 'critical' in autonomous fallback) originate in Safety Services but are enforced by Teleoperation. If Teleoperation duplicates safety logic or Safety Services duplicates failover logic, diffusion increases. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface carries constraint definitions as data (e.g., JSON schema of critical functions); Teleoperation queries and applies constraints without authoring policy. Establish clear ownership: Safety Services defines 'what is critical'; Teleoperation implements 'how to preserve critical functions during link loss'.

Risk: Vehicle module must execute both remote-commanded tasks and autonomous fallback actions. If Teleoperation duplicates vehicle control logic (e.g., motion planning, obstacle avoidance), diffusion increases. Mitigation: Teleoperation sends high-level directives (e.g., 'execute queued task X' or 'enter autonomous fallback mode Y') to Vehicle; Vehicle owns all low-level actuation, dynamics, and safety-critical control. Establish clear boundary: Teleoperation orchestrates *when* and *what*; Vehicle executes *how*.

Risk: Network Link interface abstracts physical redundant pathways. If Teleoperation also manages physical failover hardware (e.g., switching relays, network cards), diffusion increases. Mitigation: Network Link provider (external or platform layer) owns physical failover; Teleoperation owns logical failover orchestration (selecting which pathway to use based on quality metrics). Teleoperation must not duplicate network infrastructure management.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural and correct architectural home for DP5. All three pillars of the DP (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) are intrinsic to remote control capability and cohesively belong in a single module. The module boundary is clean: Teleoperation encapsulates communication assurance; Vehicle encapsulates actuation; Safety Services encapsulates policy. External interfaces are well-defined and minimize coupling. Minor diffusion risks are manageable via interface contracts and clear ownership boundaries (noted above). No recommended relocation of DP5 to other modules.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Formalize the SafetyAlert interface contract. Define a schema for 'critical function definitions' (e.g., which vehicle functions must remain executable in autonomous fallback mode). Example: {"critical_functions": ["emergency_stop", "collision_avoidance", "return_to_base"], "safety_constraints": [...]}. This prevents Teleoperation from duplicating safety logic and ensures Safety Services retains ownership of policy.

Revision 2: Clarify the Tasking interface contract. Specify how Teleoperation queues remote operator commands during link loss and executes them upon restoration. Define queue semantics (FIFO, priority-based, time-windowed) and command format. Example: {"command_id": "...", "priority": "critical|normal", "timestamp": "...", "payload": {...}}. This prevents ambiguity about command ordering and execution during failover.

Revision 3: Document the Network Link interface abstraction. Specify what 'redundant communication pathways' means in the context of the target deployment (e.g., cellular + satellite, WiFi + LTE, wired + wireless). Define the interface contract: what metrics does Network Link expose (signal strength, latency, packet loss rate, estimated time to failure)? What failover triggers does Teleoperation control? This prevents diffusion of network management logic.

Revision 4: Add acceptance criteria for autonomous fallback mode. The FR specifies link availability ≥ 0.999, but does not specify how long the system can operate in autonomous fallback mode or what functions remain available. Recommend: 'During link loss, critical functions (as defined by Safety Services) remain executable for at least [duration] or until link restoration, whichever is sooner.' This clarifies the scope of DP5 pillar 3.

Revision 5: Establish a test plan that validates the FR->DP->IM chain. Recommend: (1) Availability measurement test: run system for evaluation period, measure link availability against 0.999 target; (2) Failover test: simulate link degradation, verify automatic rerouting to redundant pathway; (3) Autonomous fallback test: simulate link loss, verify critical functions remain executable, verify seamless resumption upon restoration; (4) Diffusion test: verify Teleoperation does not duplicate Vehicle control logic, Safety Services policy, or Network Link infrastructure management. This ensures DP5 is correctly realized in Teleoperation IM.

decision.justification: The FR->DP->IM chain passes all gates with high confidence (FR_IM: pass, FR_DP: pass, DP_IM: pass, containment: 0.92). However, the gate results explicitly recommend five formal revisions to interface contracts, acceptance criteria, and test plans. Given the high safety criticality of this teleoperation system, these revisions must be incorporated before decomposition proceeds. Additionally, the three pillars of DP5 (redundant pathways, real-time monitoring with predictive detection, hierarchical architecture with autonomous fallback) are sufficiently complex and safety-critical to warrant explicit sub-FR decomposition. This ensures each pillar is independently verified and traced to implementation. The recommended revisions should be applied at the current level (DP and IM refinement), then sub-FRs derived to address each pillar with appropriate rigor.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Maintain communication via redundant pathways with automatic failover to preserve link availability during primary pathway degradation.
FR5.2: Detect link quality degradation and predict imminent link loss with sufficient lead time to trigger preventive failover or autonomous fallback.
FR5.3: Execute critical vehicle functions autonomously during link loss, preserving safety constraints defined by Safety Services, until link restoration or operator intervention.

revise.why: Before decomposing into sub-FRs, apply Revisions 1-5 from gate results: (1) Formalize SafetyAlert interface contract with critical function schema; (2) Clarify Tasking interface contract with queue semantics and command format; (3) Document Network Link interface abstraction and failover trigger definitions; (4) Add acceptance criteria for autonomous fallback mode duration and function availability; (5) Establish test plan validating FR->DP->IM chain including availability measurement, failover, autonomous fallback, and diffusion tests. These revisions ensure DP5 is fully specified and IM boundaries are unambiguous before sub-FR decomposition. This prevents rework downstream and ensures safety-critical requirements are traceable to implementation.

assumptions: Teleoperation module is the correct architectural home for all three pillars of DP5 (redundancy, monitoring, fallback); no relocation recommended.
Network Link provider (external or platform layer) owns physical failover infrastructure; Teleoperation owns logical failover orchestration.
Safety Services retains ownership of critical function definitions; Teleoperation applies constraints without authoring policy.
Vehicle module owns all low-level actuation, dynamics, and safety-critical control; Teleoperation sends high-level directives only.
Link availability ≥ 0.999 is measurable over an agreed evaluation period via continuous monitoring and failover testing.
Autonomous fallback mode is feasible and safe given Safety Services constraint definitions and Vehicle autonomous capabilities.
Predictive degradation detection logic will be encapsulated in Teleoperation and exposed only as high-level alerts to prevent diffusion.
open_risks: Diffusion risk: Predictive degradation detection logic could be duplicated across Teleoperation, Vehicle autonomous navigation, and Safety Services anomaly detection. Mitigation: Encapsulate in Teleoperation; expose via Telemetry interface; consider shared Analytics module if needed.

Diffusion risk: Safety constraint definitions could be duplicated between Safety Services and Teleoperation. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface carries constraints as data; Teleoperation applies without authoring policy.
Diffusion risk: Vehicle control logic could be duplicated between Teleoperation and Vehicle module. Mitigation: Clear boundary—Teleoperation orchestrates 'when' and 'what'; Vehicle executes 'how'.

Diffusion risk: Network infrastructure management could be duplicated between Teleoperation and Network Link provider. Mitigation: Network Link provider owns physical failover; Teleoperation owns logical orchestration.
Safety risk: Autonomous fallback mode must preserve critical functions without operator oversight. Acceptance criteria and test plan must validate this rigorously.
Integration risk: Tasking interface queue semantics (FIFO, priority, time-windowing) must be clearly specified to prevent command loss or reordering during failover.
Verification risk: Link availability measurement over evaluation period requires continuous monitoring infrastructure; test plan must specify monitoring scope and accuracy.
evidence_needed: Formalized SafetyAlert interface contract with critical function schema (JSON example provided in Revision 1).
Formalized Tasking interface contract with queue semantics and command format (JSON example provided in Revision 2).
Network Link interface abstraction specification: redundant pathway definitions, exposed metrics (signal strength, latency, packet loss, ETTF), failover trigger definitions.
Acceptance criteria for autonomous fallback mode: minimum duration of critical function availability, function list, link restoration behavior.
Test plan with five validation scenarios: (1) availability measurement, (2) failover under degradation, (3) autonomous fallback execution, (4) seamless resumption, (5) diffusion verification.
Safety analysis: FMEA or HARA for autonomous fallback mode, identifying failure modes and mitigation strategies.
Architecture diagram showing Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services, Network Link, and interface contracts with data flow.
Traceability matrix: FR5 -> DP5 -> IM (Teleoperation) -> sub-FRs (FR5.1, FR5.2, FR5.3) -> sub-DPs -> sub-IMs.
